GDPR & FACEBOOK ADS
Updated: 21st May 2018
GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) is a European initiative aimed at bringing data privacy
laws up to date in the digital world we now live in.
From 25th May 2018 all companies trading with consumers/customers/clients in the EU were
required to become compliant.
The following video is compiled based on:
Previous work I conducted for a global UK based company, Tesco, in 2010-2011 in order to
help them become 'Cookie privacy' compliant as part of a contract role in building their
digital personalisation engine.
Liaising with Facebook's legal counsel, reading through Facebook's material on GDPR and
questions sent to answered by my agency rep.
Attending webinars, reading blog posts and guides on GDPR in general
NONE OF THE INFORMATION HERE CONSTITUTES LEGAL ADVICE.
Use the information solely for the benefit of gaining a broader understanding of your obligations
with regards to Facebook Ads.
If in doubt, seek out legal counsel in your local country.
00:00 / 0:00

ADVICE FOR AGENCIES
As an agency owner, I have stipulated to clients that THEY are responsible for uploading all email
lists into Facebook AND they are also responsible for complying with GDPR in supplying those lists
and that we waive all responsibilities as a Data Controller or Data Processing AND that they Client
and Facebook are bound into agreement for the purposes of Custom Audiences.
Some agencies decided to continue handling client email lists in which case they are putting
themselves into the position of Data Controller (depending on where those leads go) and Data
Processor (depending on what they do with those emails) which can create additional responsibility.
My position on this, again with no legal advice intended from this, is to simply place full
responsibility with the client.
As an agency advertising on behalf of a client, it is your responsibility to protect your client's
interests, to ensure they are fully aware of compliance to work with Facebook ads and whilst the
above will mitigate custom audiences, you are still partly responsible for the pixel as the person
running ads for the client.
PIXEL AND AUDIENCE SHARING
If you are running 2 accounts that you own and decide to share pixels or audiences, Facebook's new
terms actually state that you need to seek their permission to do so. From the 25th May 2018 I
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 3 --

would suggest doing this, even if you own both accounts and it points to a single website. In most
cases, you'll be right to think 'who will ever know' - on the flip-side a data breach could cost you 4%
of your annual revenue (not profit) so its your choice
I highly recommend AGAINST sharing pixel and customer data across two unassociated accounts,
for example as a business partnership as this will directly be against Facebook's terms and also
cause you potential issue with regard to GDPR.
CUSTOM AUDIENCE DATA
Facebook have confirmed that data you upload (emails, mobile numbers) to Facebook as an
advertiser for the purpose of creating a Custom Audience, is hashed (secured) and the original file
destroyed. This means Facebook doesn't keep your uploaded files and the data they have is
encrypted so they can't see the data. This protects the consumers privacy.
OFFICIAL FACEBOOK Q&A
I. GENERAL
Q: What is the GDPR?
A: The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is a new framework that creates consistent data
protection rules across the European Union (EU). The regulations will apply to companies globally
— both those based in the EU and those who interact with individuals in the EU — and will apply
starting May 25, 2018. While many of the principles build on current data protection rules in the EU,
the GDPR places some new requirements on companies. It also adds significant enforcement
authority, giving a company's lead supervisory authority the ability to seek fines of up to 4% of
global annual revenues for certain violations.
Q: What is Facebook's position on GDPR?
A: Facebook takes data protection seriously and we comply with data protection laws that apply to
us. We will of course ensure that our services align with the GDPR. We’ve built tools to help people
manage their privacy and understand their choices with respect to their personal data. We
appreciate that the GDPR requires our advertisers and business partners, when acting as data
controllers, to make sure Facebook (acting as the data processor) has the appropriate safeguards in
place. We are committed to those safeguards and will meet those requirements.
Q: How does GDPR impact Instagram ads?
A: Facebook and its affiliates, including Instagram, Oculus and WhatsApp, will all comply with the
GDPR. With respect to your ads on Instagram, Facebook operates the advertising service that shows
ads on Instagram.
Q: How are we responding to incoming client questionnaires about Facebook's readiness and
compliance?
A: Given the volume of Facebook advertisers, we are unable to respond to individual client
questionnaires at this time. We encourage sales teams to consult these FAQs in order to provide
more information to clients about Facebook's GDPR preparation.
II. FACEBOOK DATA & CLIENT DATA
Q: Does Facebook see itself as a Data Controller, Data Processor or both? For each, can you provide
detail in which instances these relate to?
A: Both, depending on the circumstances. Below, we've outlined details about Facebook's role in
each of these designations. Data controller: In most cases, the Facebook Companies (Facebook and
Messenger, Instagram, Oculus and WhatsApp), will be acting as a data controller. When Facebook is
the data controller, we handle personal data as described in our Data Policy. For example, Facebook
is the data controller of most on-Facebook activity. Facebook Companies (such as WhatsApp and
Oculus) each handle personal data as described in their own data policies. We will ensure that
services across the Facebook family align with GDPR, which may involve making available new tools
to users and reviewing existing tools to make sure we honor our obligations.Data processor: In
certain instances, Facebook acts as a data processor on behalf of advertisers or business partners
(the data controller), such as for Data File Custom Audiences and Workplace Premium. There are
specific compliance requirements for data processors that we'll make sure we comply with. For
example, we've updated the contractual obligations that must be agreed upon between data
controllers and data processors to align with the updated GDPR requirements.
Q: When an advertiser is using Facebook data to target customers, is Facebook the data controller?
A: Facebook is a data controller when we use our own data (e.g. interests we have derived based on
what people do on Facebook) to show advertisers' ads to people.
Q: What's changing in Facebook's updated product terms and how will this impact businesses?
A: Based on feedback from businesses, we're consolidating some of our product terms so that you
can find more terms in one place. We are introducing the term Facebook Business Tools, which
refers to the tools that we provide to help website owners and publishers, developers, advertisers,
business partners (and their customers) and others integrate, use and exchange information with
Facebook. The Facebook Business Tools include APIs and SDKs, the Facebook Pixel, social plugins
such as the Like and Share buttons, Facebook Login and Account Kit, as well as our measurement
and analytics products and other platform integrations, plugins, code, specifications,
documentation, technology and services. Additionally, we are making updates to our Custom
Audience Terms to clarify that businesses are responsible for their own relationships with
independent data providers. Finally, we have made updates to our terms in connection with our
compliance with the EU's General Data Protection Regulation. For example, we now clarify when we
are acting as a data processor versus data controller, and we have added a data processing
addendum for advertisers who are processing personal data associated with EU users.
Q: If an advertiser is targeting customers on Facebook based on a cookie on their site, would this fall
under GDPR?
A: The processing of personal data is regulated by the GDPR; the collection of data via cookies and
the use of Facebook's advertising tools ordinarily involve processing of personal data, and as such
will have to comply with GDPR to the extent that an EU user data is involved.
Q: If an ad is placed on Facebook using personal information provided by advertisers, whose
consent will you rely on for this?
A: Facebook is the data controller when it shows ads to people based on the information people
provide directly to Facebook and for data Facebook receives when websites and apps install
Facebook's pixel and SDX. In these cases, we are responsible for ensuring compliance including by
providing notice and establishing a legal basis to process that data. If you’re using Facebook’s data
file Custom Audience product to reach your customers or using Facebook's measurement and
analytics services and providing EU users' personal information to us, Facebook is acting as a data
processor you must ensure compliance for our processing of the personal data to provide services
for you. Note: legal bases are the legal justifications companies have to process data for delivering
their services. There are three legal bases that Facebook will likely rely upon: consent, contractual
necessity and legitimate interests. Companies must have at least one legal basis for each kind of
data processing, but they can rely on different bases for different kinds of processing. For example, if
Facebook can rely on legitimate interests to justify our ability to serve targeted ads, we do not need
to require consumers' affirmative consent (i.e. the 'consent' legal basis). One example of affirmative
consent is having someone click “accept” on a pop up window before receiving an ad. For the most
part, the legitimate interests' legal basis won’t change significantly under GDPR. Companies need to
carefully consider the balance between their legitimate interests and the interests of the individual
and how they document such interests. Any assessment will necessarily be on a case by case basis,
depending on the company and the context. The legitimate interests basis is also necessity-based
and is only applicable to the extent that the processing is strictly necessary for the purpose of the
company’s legitimate interests. Therefore, companies should consider whether there is any less
invasive processing that would allow them to still reach their business goals. Contractual necessity
can be a high bar for companies to reach and essentially means that the processing must be
essential to the service being provided by the advertiser. If such processing isn’t obvious to the end
user, there’s a high risk that it wouldn’t be considered necessary for the purposes of the contract.
But again, this will always be determined on a case by case basis depending on the company and
the context.
Q: What data is the Pixel collecting?
A: Details about the cookies we use and the data collected can be found in the detailed table in
our Cookie Policy. Facebook's pixel collects three types of data whenever it is loaded: Http
Headers – Anything present in HTTP headers. HTTP Headers is a standard web protocol sent by
default between any browser request and any server on the internet. HTTP Headers include IP
addresses, information about the web browser, page location, document, referrer, and user agent.
You can read more about HTTP Headers here.Pixel-specific Data – This includes Pixel ID and the
Facebook Cookie.Optional Values – Developers and marketers can optionally choose to send
additional information about the visit through Custom Data events. Example custom data events
are conversion value, page type, and more. Read more about custom data events here.
Q: Will Facebook's plug-in functionality be impacted?
A: Our GDPR team is still reviewing plug in functionality, and we will keep you updated if any
product changes arise from this review.
Q: How long will we save the data in our system and when are we deleting data?
A: We include a detailed table within our Cookies policy, specifying the expiration of each cookie
Facebook sets. We inform users about the cookies on their browsers through our
Facebook Data and Cookies Policies. Also, we require our advertisers to provide sufficient notice to
and gain all necessary consent from their users for their use of the Facebook products and the
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 3 --

collection of data in our Terms, which includes a link to Facebook's Cookies Consent Guide for sites
and apps. Like today's policy, the GDPR requires that data is only held for so long as is necessary for
the purposes for which it was collected, and that data subjects are informed of the retention period
or the criteria by which the retention period will be determined. Facebook will continue to comply
with these requirements.
Q: Is this data being stored on European servers?
A: We operate a global infrastructure and process data in both EU and US-based servers. This
processing is supported by strict legal compliance for safeguarding any transfers of personal data
outside of the EU. Facebook has certified certain products for which it acts as data processor under
Privacy Shield, as explained further in its Privacy Shield announcement and certification
here: Privacy Shield Announcement and Certification.
Q: How will the GDPR impact the existing third party measurement and FMP ecosystem?
A: We're working with our measurement partners and FMPs to ensure compliance with GDPR. At this
time, we expect the vast majority of our partnerships will continue uninterrupted.
III. USING FACEBOOK ADVERTISING PRODUCTS IN COMPLIANCE WITH GDPR
Q: Under GDPR, do we foresee any restrictions in the way brands use our platform and solutions?
A: When an advertiser is the data controller (e.g. data file custom audiences), they must ensure
compliance with applicable law, including ensuring a relevant legal basis (for example, consent,
contractual necessity or legitimate interests). Brands can continue to use Facebook platforms and
solutions in the same way, but they are responsible for ensuring compliance with the applicable
GDPR rules. We foresee a notable area of impact being any product or service where Facebook acts
as data processor, and where our advertisers and business partners act as data controllers,
including Data file Custom Audiences and Workplace Premium.
Q: How will GDPR impact Pixel? What do advertisers need to do to implement Pixel?
A: Where you use our pixel, you will have obligations under the GDPR. Our terms provide that
companies implementing our tools must comply with applicable laws when they use our tools. For
companies operating in the EU, this includes obtaining prior informed consent for the storing and
access of cookies or other information on the end user's device. We offer a consent guide for sites
and apps that provides some practical guidance and best practice for these consent requirements.
Q: How will Custom Audiences be affected by GDPR and will advertisers need to make any changes
on their end in order to continue using these solutions?
A: Advertisers today and under GDPR act as the data controller of any customer lists uploaded as
part of data file custom audiences. They are responsible for ensuring compliance with applicable
law for using that data. This means, among other things, that you must have a clear legal basis for
collecting the data (for example, consent, contractual necessity or legitimate interest). As the data
processor acting on your behalf, Facebook is processing the hashed data you upload and use to
generate custom audiences according to the relevant legal basis you rely on as data controller.
Note that Facebook's terms require advertisers to ensure they have a lawful basis to upload and
use the data you provide in connection with Custom Audiences; requirements may vary depending
on the laws that apply to you.
Q: How will Lead Ads be affected by GDPR?
A: In the case of lead ads, both Facebook and the business are data controllers, thus, both parties
are responsible for ensuring compliance by providing notice and establishing a legal basis for
processing the data provided by a user. The lead ads product offers advertisers the ability to link to
their privacy policies and terms related to the collection and use of user data. Q: How will Offline
Conversions be impacted by GDPR? A: We have updated our product terms so that it is clear when
an advertiser is acting as a data controller vs. data processor in the case of offline conversions. When
processing your offline conversions data to provide measurement or analytics reports, Facebook is
acting as a data processor, and an advertiser must acquire the appropriate legal basis for our
processing of this data. Q: How should advertisers think about Lookalike Audiences in the context
of GDPR? A: When an advertiser creates a Lookalike Audience, they choose a source audience - this
can be a data file Custom Audience, or a Custom Audience created with a data partner, pixel or
mobile app data or fans of their Page. Facebook then identifies the common qualities of the people
in it (e.g. demographic information or interests) and finds people who are similar to (or "look like")
them. Facebook is the data controller for the data that is processed to create the Lookalike
audience. The audience is constantly changing and is unique to the source audience given the
characteristics of the users who make up the advertiser's custom audience. Lookalike audiences
should be thought of as groups of characteristics and interests into which some users will come and
go, it is not a fixed audience of Facebook users. Millions of features are considered when placing
users in a Lookalike audience. Again, these Lookalike audiences are comprised of Facebook users
and Facebook is the data controller for this data, not the advertiser. Q: Does Facebook recommend
any tools to help advertisers with GDPR compliance? A: We recommend that businesses consult
with their legal, policy and/or compliance teams to determine whether there are third-party
solutions to fit their needs. IV. PEOPLE'S RIGHTS & CONSENTS Q: How is Facebook tackling subject
access requests? A: Facebook has been directly subject to European data protection law, including
the right of access, since 2010. We offer a range of tools for people to access, correct and transfer
their data. For example, Download Your Information and Activity Log, as well as other in-product
information. We anticipate offering features like these across our apps and services in compliance
with the GDPR. Q: How you are dealing with digital personal identifiers under GDPR and are you
applying subject access right to these? A: We evaluate each piece of data we hold to understand
how the legal regime applies and ensure that we meet our compliance obligations where
applicable. We already provide access to many identifiers via our Download Your Information tool
and other features and anticipate continuing to provide access as required by GDPR. Q: How will
Facebook re-certify permissions of its community to continue to advertise on the platform? A:
Facebook has updated its terms and policies in connection with GDPR. Facebook will ensure that it
has an appropriate legal basis for the processing of EU user data under the GDPR.
